source_paper_title: Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Srikant_2025, title={Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Srikant, R.}, year={2025}, month=oct }
context:
Section 5 (Conclusions), page 19

Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.
title: Improve Wasserstein convergence rate for vector martingale central limit theorem
background_latex:
Let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\) be a \(d\)-dimensional martingale difference sequence with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\), meaning \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-measurable, \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|<\infty\), and \(\mathbb E[m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]=0\) for all \(k\ge 1\). Define partial sums \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) and the normalized sum \(W_n=S_n/\sqrt n\).

Assume there exists \(\beta\in(0,1)\) such that \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) for all \(k\), and the conditional covariance matrices \(\Sigma_k:=\mathbb E[m_k m_k^\top\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]\) exist. Let \(\Sigma_\infty\succ 0\) be a positive definite matrix intended to represent the limiting (asymptotic) covariance of \(W_n\) in a martingale central limit theorem.

Let \(Z\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d)\). For random vectors \(X,Y\in\mathbb R^d\), the \(1\)-Wasserstein distance is
\[
 d_W(X,Y)=\sup_{h\in\mathrm{Lip}_1} \mathbb E[h(X)-h(Y)],
\]
where \(\mathrm{Lip}_1\) is the set of real-valued functions on \(\mathbb R^d\) with Lipschitz constant at most 1 w.r.t. the Euclidean norm.

A non-asymptotic martingale CLT bound in Wasserstein distance can have the form
\[
 d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr) \le \text{(explicit bound depending on moments, }\Sigma_k,\Sigma_\infty\text{)}.
\]
In the paper, a general bound is proved that (in typical applications) yields the rate \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) in \(d_W\).
problem_definition_latex:
Find additional, verifiable conditions on the martingale difference sequence \(\{m_k\}\) (beyond the finite-\((2+\beta)\) moment and conditional covariance assumptions stated above) under which the Wasserstein error
\[
 d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr)
\]
admits an improved rate of convergence \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (i.e., with the \(\log n\) factor removed) as \(n\to\infty\), while keeping a vector-valued martingale setting and a Gaussian limit with covariance \(\Sigma_\infty\).
latex:

Find additional, verifiable conditions on the martingale difference sequence \(\{m_k\}\) (beyond the finite-\((2+\beta)\) moment and conditional covariance assumptions stated above) under which the Wasserstein error
\[
 d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr)

admits an improved rate of convergence \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (i.e., with the \(\log n\) factor removed) as \(n\to\infty\), while keeping a vector-valued martingale setting and a Gaussian limit with covariance \(\Sigma_\infty\).
open_problem_quote: Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for martingale difference arrays where the Wasserstein distance decays like \(c\log n/\sqrt n\) or \(c/\sqrt n\) by simulating candidate constructions and estimating \(d_W\) numerically, but accurate Wasserstein estimation in moderate dimension is nontrivial and noisy, making automated counterexample discovery only moderately feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate sufficient conditions or refined bounds can be explored by generating parametric families of martingales (e.g., functions of Markov chains via Poisson equations) and numerically regressing the decay of \(d_W\) as a fitness proxy; however, the search space over "conditions" is not directly codable, and the main bottleneck is computing a reliable Wasserstein-distance proxy, so suitability is moderate rather than high.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
